title: Dyeing with hollyhock
subtitle: Blue-purple to grey-green from deep-purple hollyhock petals on pre-mordanted wool
excerpt: Deep-purple hollyhock petals (Alcea rosea) produce an unusually blue or grey-purple dye on alum-mordanted wool. The colour is sensitive to pH and can shift from blue-purple to grey-green with a vinegar modifier. Hollyhock is one of the rare blue-tending natural dye plants available from a garden.
sourceNotes: Hollyhock as a natural dye documented in Jenny Dean, Wild Colour (1999, in-copyright, substance only referenced), and in historical plant-dye references. The blue-purple colouring from deep-red hollyhock petals is well established in British natural-dye teaching.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt that bonds the dye to the wool fibre. Alum gives a blue-purple result from hollyhock; iron shifts the colour toward grey-green.
The extracted petal dye liquid in which the mordanted fibre is placed.

An acid (such as vinegar) or alkali (such as washing soda) added to a dye bath to shift the colour. Hollyhock colour is pH-sensitive: acid conditions push toward blue-purple; alkaline conditions push toward green.

content.text: Hollyhock gives blue-purple dye from its dark petals. The colour comes from pigments that shift with
must stay cool and slightly acid to keep the blue tone. Use only the deepest-purple petals with an alum
. Pale pink petals do not give a reliable colour.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Dye-pots and utensils are dedicated. Wear gloves and apron. Work outdoors or with ventilation. Alum is low-hazard; keep away from food.

items.name: Deep-purple or black-purple hollyhock petals (fresh or dried)
White vinegar (to acidify the dye bath)

content.text: Step 1: Mordant the fibre at lower temperature'
Mordant the wool with alum at 75 degrees Celsius (not the usual 80) for 45 minutes. Hollyhock colour is heat-sensitive; even the mordanting step benefits from slightly lower temperatures.
Step 2: Extract the dye at low temperature'

Place the petals in the dye-pot with water to cover. Add 2 tablespoons of white vinegar to the water before heating. Heat slowly to 70 degrees Celsius and hold for 30 minutes only; do not boil. Overheating destroys the anthocyanin compounds and shifts the colour from blue-purple to brown. Strain the cooled bath through cloth.
Step 3: Dye the fibre at low temperature'

Add the mordanted, damp fibre to the cooled dye bath (no hotter than 65 to 70 degrees Celsius). Hold at this temperature for 45 minutes. Remove the fibre and rinse in cool water of the same temperature. Avoid temperature shock; sudden temperature change can cause felting in wool.
The alum mordant bath and hollyhock dye bath can be composted or poured down the foul drain in small home quantities.

Replacing the vinegar with washing soda (sodium carbonate) in the dye bath shifts the colour toward grey-green. The alkali changes the pigment structure. Add soda ash in very small amounts and test on a sample first; the shift can be dramatic.

items.problem: The colour came out brown rather than blue-purple
items.cause: The dye bath was overheated, breaking down the anthocyanin pigments
items.fix: Hollyhock anthocyanins degrade above about 80 degrees Celsius. Keep the dye bath at 70 degrees Celsius maximum and check frequently.

items.problem: The blue-purple colour faded completely after a few weeks
items.cause: Anthocyanin dyes are not lightfast; hollyhock colour is fugitive in sunlight
items.fix: Use hollyhock-dyed textiles indoors and away from direct sun. All anthocyanin dyes fade in sunlight. This is true of elderberry, blackberry, and hollyhock alike. No mordant makes them lightfast.
